title: AI Coding Tools for Software Teams

modules.title: The AI-Augmented Developer: Productivity Realities Beyond the Hype
modules.sub: Separating evidence-based productivity gains from vendor claims
modules.description: Learners examine real-world productivity research and case studies to build a grounded picture of what AI coding tools actually deliver. They identify which task types see meaningful gains, which see marginal ones, and where over-reliance introduces risk. The module equips engineering leads to set realistic expectations before any tooling decision is made.

modules.title: Tool Landscape: Copilot, Cursor, Codeium, and the Category Map
modules.sub: Mapping the major players, architectures, and differentiating trade-offs
modules.description: Learners survey the current AI coding tool landscape and develop a framework for comparing products across dimensions such as model quality, IDE integration, data privacy posture, and cost. They apply the framework to leading tools including GitHub Copilot, Cursor, and Codeium. By the end, they can produce a defensible shortlist tailored to their team's context.

modules.title: Integrating AI Tools into Existing Engineering Workflows and CI/CD Pipelines
modules.sub: Fitting AI assistance into real pipelines without disrupting delivery flow
modules.description: Learners map their existing engineering workflow touchpoints and identify where AI assistance adds value versus where it creates friction or risk. They design integration patterns for code generation, review, and pipeline automation that complement rather than bypass existing quality gates. The module results in a concrete integration plan ready for piloting.

modules.title: Code Review, Quality, and the Problem of Plausible-But-Wrong Suggestions
modules.sub: Keeping human judgment sharp when AI output looks convincing
modules.description: Learners analyze how AI-generated code can be syntactically correct and superficially plausible while containing logic errors, edge-case failures, or subtle regressions. They design updated code review checklists and team norms that account for the specific failure modes AI suggestions introduce. The module builds habits that preserve code quality without eliminating the productivity benefit of AI assistance.

modules.title: Security Implications: Training Data, IP Exposure, and Autocompleted Vulnerabilities
modules.sub: Understanding and controlling the security risks AI coding tools introduce
modules.description: Learners examine how AI coding tools can expose proprietary code to third-party training pipelines, reproduce license-encumbered snippets, and autocomplete known vulnerability patterns such as injection flaws or insecure defaults. They assess their organization's risk profile and define acceptable-use policies and technical guardrails to mitigate those risks. The module produces a security checklist suitable for inclusion in an AI tool governance policy.

modules.title: Team Adoption Strategies: Rollout, Measurement, and Managing Skepticism
modules.sub: Rolling out AI tools as a team capability, not an individual experiment
modules.description: Learners design a phased adoption plan that accounts for varying skill levels, role differences, and developer skepticism. They select leading and lagging metrics to track whether the rollout is delivering value and identify early warning signs of misuse or disengagement. The module produces an adoption roadmap with stakeholder communication guidance built in.

modules.title: AI-Assisted Testing, Documentation, and Legacy Code Modernization
modules.sub: Applying AI assistance to high-value tasks beyond feature code generation
modules.description: Learners explore how AI tools can accelerate test generation, documentation upkeep, and the particularly high-friction work of understanding and modernizing legacy codebases. They evaluate prompt strategies and workflow patterns that produce reliable output in each of these contexts. The module results in practical playbooks their teams can adopt immediately for each use case.

modules.title: Leading an AI-Augmented Engineering Team: Norms, Metrics, and the Evolving Role of the Developer
modules.sub: Defining what great engineering leadership looks like in an AI-assisted era
modules.description: Learners synthesize the course into a team-level AI strategy document that covers tool selection rationale, governance norms, quality and security guardrails, and success metrics aligned to organizational goals. They reflect on how the role of the developer and the responsibilities of the engineering manager are shifting as AI handles more routine coding work. The module prepares leads to communicate and champion their strategy upward to leadership and outward to their teams.

synopsis: AI coding assistants are reshaping what software teams can ship and how they work â but adoption without strategy creates technical debt, security exposure, and uneven productivity gains. This course equips engineering managers and senior developers to evaluate, deploy, and govern AI coding tools as a deliberate team capability rather than an individual perk.
rationale: Engineering teams are adopting AI coding tools rapidly and inconsistently, and most engineering managers lack the frameworks to govern them, measure their impact, or address the security and quality risks they introduce.
learning_outcome: Engineering leads can design and implement a team-level AI coding tool strategy â covering tool selection, rollout, quality guardrails, and success metrics â appropriate to their organization's risk tolerance and codebase.
